ProjectDescription: The digital contact notebook modernizes daily care tracking for children with disabilities in special daycare settings. Staff can log structured information, including meals, naps, therapy sessions, activities, and photos, while parents access real-time updates and monthly summaries through an intuitive dashboard. An AI agent analyzes patterns, identifies anomalies, and provides actionable insights to support staff decisions. Automatic reports give parents and staff clear overviews of attendance, activities, and developmental trends. An optional background Google Speach-to-Text service enriches operational inputs, improving transparency, care quality, and developmental monitoring for every child.

Lists.Name: Sprint 8
Lists.Description:
This is the final stabilization push. Your mission is to switch from feature building to a collaborative "Bug Hunting" phase alongside the entire team to ensure the platform is production-ready. 
 
 You are moving from the "feature" phase to the "quality" phase. This sprint is entirely focused on identifying glitches and ensuring the user experience is flawless before the final handoff. Since everyone is doing QA, you will monitor the Meeting Room for every "Bugs case" logged and address them in your dedicated branch. 
 
 Success means a pixel-perfect, error-free frontend that has been fully merged into the main repository.

Cards.Name: Infrastructure Audit & High-Level Data Modeling
Cards.Description:
Welcome to the team! 
 
 This week is your "Technical Landing": a low-pressure space to explore the project's foundations. 
 
 Your objective is to partner with the PM to translate their business journey into a technical blueprint. 
 
 You will verify the environment and create the "Big Picture" database model that will serve as our project's technical map.
ChecklistItems: [ ] Team Kickoff: Participate in the "Project Kickoff" to meet your teammates and understand the project’s high-level mission.
[ ] Local Repository Setup: Clone the provided GitHub repository to your local developer environment to begin your technical exploration.
[ ] Sprint Branch Initialization (Mentor Panel): Use the mentor buttons panel to create your official branch for the current sprint.
[ ] Local Sync Verification (Local Environment): From your local environment, push a "Health Check" commit to your branch to confirm your local IDE is perfectly synced with the repository.

[ ] GitHub Cycle Practice (Mentor Panel): Return to the mentor buttons panel to create a Pull Request (PR) and merge your branch into main. Use this to gain a hands-on understanding of the project's GitHub cycle.
[ ] Infrastructure Audit: Connect to the Postgres DB and perform a "Stress Test" (e.g., creating and deleting a dummy table) to ensure the environment is stable for future data.

[ ] Collaborative Journey & Persona Review: Sit down with the PM to review their Global Journey Diagram and review the UI/UX Designer's new User Personas. Use this session to brainstorm the core entities and relationships (e.g., Donors, Coordinators, Drivers) needed to support the entire system.
[ ] High-Level Schema Diagram: Based on your review with the PM, create a visual ERD (Entity Relationship Diagram) showing the high-level data architecture.
[ ] Blueprint Centralization: Save your schema diagram in the Meeting Room so the team can reference our "Data Source of Truth." (Your Mentor can guide you on the process).

Cards.Name: Frontend Orientation & Environmental Foundation
Cards.Description:
Welcome to the front line! 
 
 We are thrilled to have you on board. 
 
 This first week is your "Technical Landing" - a low-pressure space to explore your new digital playground. 
 
 While the PM and UI/UX teams define the project's soul, your objective is to ensure your development environment is perfectly tuned and to collaborate on the project's global structure. 
 
 You are the team's bridge between design and code.
ChecklistItems: [ ] Team Kickoff: Participate in the "Sprint 1 Kickoff" to meet the team and align on our high-level mission.
[ ] Local Repository Setup: Clone the provided GitHub repository to your local developer environment to begin your technical setup.
[ ] Sprint Branch Initialization (Mentor Panel): Use the mentor buttons panel to create your official branch for the current sprint.

[ ] Local Sync Verification (Local Environment): From your local environment, push a "Health Check" commit to your branch to confirm your local IDE and build tools are perfectly synced with the repository.

[ ] GitHub Cycle Practice (Mentor Panel): Return to the mentor buttons panel to create a Pull Request (PR) and merge your branch into main. Use this to gain a hands-on understanding of the project's GitHub cycle.
[ ] Tech Stack Audit: Explore the project's frontend boilerplate (React/Next.js/etc.) and verify that all necessary dependencies and linting rules are correctly installed.
[ ] Collaborative Journey Review: Sit down with the PM and the Backend Developer to review the Global Journey Diagram. Use this to identify potential UI complexities or navigation challenges early.

[ ] Global Styling Foundation: Based on the UI/UX Designer's initial research, begin initializing the project's "Foundations"—set up the global CSS variables for the color palette, typography scales, and spacing units.

Cards.Name: Ecosystem & CRM Foundation
Cards.Description:
Welcome to the team!

We are so excited to have you as our "Market Intelligence" lead.

This first week is a low-pressure opportunity for you to learn about the industry we are entering.

Your mission is to map out the "playing field" - the ecosystem of a Daycare daily operation for children with disabilities - and identify who the key players are.

Think of the AI Mentor as your research consultant who will hold your hand while you learn our internal CRM module.

This week is all about discovery and building a solid knowledge base for the team.
ChecklistItems: Team Kickoff: Join the "Sprint 1 Kickoff" to meet the team and understand our shared starting point.
Anchor Alignment: Start an introductory conversation with the AI Customer to understand the business world they operate in every day.
CRM & Research Setup: Ask the AI Mentor for a step-by-step walkthrough of the CRM module and for professional guidance on creating a "Stakeholder Map."

Conduct desk research on special daycare centers for children with disabilities to understand how daily care activities, therapy sessions, attendance, and communication with parents are recorded, monitored, and shared.
Competitive Analysis: Review 2-3 existing global solutions and summarize how they approach this industry.

Stakeholder Mapping: Identify key stakeholders in special daycare settings, including caregivers, parents, therapists, etc. and document these groups in the CRM to understand their roles, needs, and interactions within the care environment.
NSM Prep: Get familiar with the term NSM (Next Sprint Meeting); you’ll share your ecosystem findings here to help the team stay grounded in the industry's reality.

Cards.Name: Vision, Discovery & The PRD Foundation
Cards.Description:
Welcome to the helm! 
 
 Your mission this week is to transform initial ambiguity into a solid strategic roadmap. 
 
 You are the Strategic Detective, digging deep into the "Why" and "Who" of this project. 
 
 The Big Picture: While we are establishing the foundations now, keep in mind that the actual construction of our first module begins in Sprint 3. 
 
 This week is about building the trust and the blueprint: the Project PRD - required to make that build successful.
ChecklistItems: Project Kickoff: Lead the official "Project Kickoff" in the meeting room. Set the vision, align the team on the mission, and facilitate introductions.
Kickoff Synthesis: Publish a concise meeting summary and a list of immediate action items in the Team Chat to ensure Day 1 alignment.
Relationship Building: Initiate introductory conversations with each teammate (BE, FE, UI/UX, BizDev) to establish a personal connection and a supportive team culture.
Team Connection Recap: Post a high-level summary of the team’s collective strengths and support needs in the Team Chat to reinforce the collaborative foundation.
PRD Initialization: Initialize the Product Requirements Document (PRD). This is the central home for the project’s DNA. Use it to document every strategic decision you make this week.

Problem Validation: Interview the AI Customer to confirm reality vs. assumptions regarding their current workflow. Document their "Pain Points" and "Success Criteria" in the Discovery section of your PRD.
Customer Insight Broadcast: Share the key takeaways and prioritized action items from the customer interview in the Team Chat to ground the team in the user's reality.
North Star & KPIs: Define the project's North Star Metric and 3–5 supporting KPIs. Add these to the PRD to serve as the decision filter for the entire journey.
Actor Alignment: Identify the main actors (e.g., Daycare staff, Parents, etc.). Map out their primary responsibilities and "Needs" within the User Persona section of the PRD.

Journey Mapping & Centralization: Create a visual diagram of the end-to-end Special Daycare Operation journey. Integrate this diagram directly into the PRD so it serves as the project’s visual anchor.
PRD Accessibility: Save the PRD in the Team Room. Ensure it is available to everyone and clearly marked as the project's "Source of Truth."
Team Journey Demonstration: Set a meeting with the entire team to demonstrate the Journey Map and walk through the Draft PRD. Gather feedback and ensure everyone sees the "Big Picture."
Vision Alignment Summary: Publish the final meeting summary and a consolidated list of feedback-driven action items in the Team Chat to officially close the loop on Sprint 1.

Cards.Name: Ecosystem Discovery & Research Foundation
Cards.Description:
Welcome to the team! 
 
 As our lead designer, you are the architect of the user's experience. 
 
 This first week is your "Discovery" phase: a low-pressure space to understand the industry and define who we are building for. 
 
 Before diving into the visuals, you will coordinate your research roadmap with the Product Manager. 
 
 Your goal is to gather the competitive intelligence and persona insights needed to shape a high-impact design strategy in the weeks ahead.
ChecklistItems: Project Kickoff: Participate in the Project Kickoff meeting to align on the mission and meet your new teammates.
Product Manager Alignment: Meet with the Product Manager to plan your specific research tasks and milestones for the remainder of the sprint.
Figma Setup: Initialize your Figma project and link it to the Meeting Room. Ask the AI Mentor if you need instructions on how to sync the files.

Market Research: Analyze existing solutions used for daycare communication, care tracking, and parent reporting. Note the advantages and limitations of each system and upload a short summary of your findings to the Meeting Room.
Visual Inspiration Board: Create a Mood Board or "Inspiration Table" in Figma. Focus on UI patterns, color psychology, and layouts that align with the project goals.

User Personas: Define initial user personas such as caregiver, parent, and therapist. Document their needs, motivations, and potential challenges. Add the persona descriptions and designs to the project’s Figma.
User Journey Insights: Review the Global User Journey created by the Product Manager. Flag any specific areas or flows that require extra UX attention.

Cards.Name: Data Architecture & Technical Readiness
Cards.Description:
This week is about definition over execution.

Your goal is to move from the abstract "Big Picture" to a precise technical blueprint for the Daycare Staff Input Experience.

You are the architect of the data flow.

By the end of this week, you will have established the plan for how the Frontend and Backend communicate.

You aren't building the final logic yet; you are preparing the "manual" so that the team can build with total confidence in Sprint 3.
ChecklistItems: Environment Launch: Launch the sprint and create your Sprint 2 branch via the mentor buttons panel to begin your technical exploration.

Tooling and Library Audit: Review your current backend stack and libraries (e.g., validation libraries, database drivers). Ensure everything is optimized and ready for the specific data-entry and form-handling tasks coming next week.

Frontend Data Sync (General Handshake): Meet with the Frontend Developer to agree on the general "Blueprint" for how data will move between the UI and the server (e.g., Auth patterns, error handling, and endpoint structure).

Requirements Sync (Tables and Fields): Once the PM has finalized the User Story, meet to identify the specific Tables and Fields required to support the Staff Input Form. Map out exactly what data points need to be stored (e.g., activity type and timestamps).

Database Schema Blueprint: Update the Database Schema diagram based on the PM's finalized fields. This acts as the "Contract" for the project's data structure and serves as the team's single source of truth.
Mock Data Provision: Generate a set of "dummy" Daycare Staff data. The goal is to give the Frontend Developer realistic data to work with in Sprint 3 before you have built the actual database logic.
Next Sprint Meeting (NSM): Participate in the NSM to present your technical blueprint. Confirm that your architecture can support the validation rules required by the customer.

Blueprint Delivery (PR and Merge): Commit your final schema designs and mock data files to your branch. Create a Pull Request (PR) and merge it into main via the mentor buttons panel to lock in the technical blueprint for the build.

Cards.Name: UI Architecture & Component Scaffolding
Cards.Description:
This week, you are the "Architect" of the interface.

Before we start coding the actual logic for the Daycare Staff Experience in Sprint 3, you will build the reusable "Atoms" that make development fast and consistent.

You are building the "lego pieces" and the "hallways" of the application.

By the end of the week, the team should be able to click through the basic navigation of the flow, even if the forms don't "save" data yet.

This is where we build the skeleton so that next week, we can add the muscle.
ChecklistItems: Environment Launch: Launch the sprint and create your Sprint 2 branch via the mentor buttons panel to begin your technical exploration.
Component Library Initialization: Set up a dedicated workspace for your reusable UI components (e.g., a /components library folder or a Storybook setup).

Atomic Component Build: Build the foundational, "logic-less" components required for the Daycare Staff Form: Buttons, Input Fields (Text/Select), Loaders, and Success Modals. Apply the Visual Language (Colors and Typography) established by the Designer this week.

Frontend Data Sync: Meet with the Backend Developer to agree on the "Data Blueprint." Confirm the specific "Request" (what the form sends) and "Response" (what the server says back) so that your components are ready for real data.

App Navigation "Hallways": Set up the empty page containers and the URL paths for the Daycare Staff flow (e.g., /submit for the form and /success for the confirmation). This ensures the structural journey is ready for the logic in Sprint 3.
UI/UX Handshake: Review the Designer’s final design. Ensure general synchronization regarding technical feasibility and confirm that the layout aligns with the UI components you are preparing.
Next Sprint Meeting (NSM): Participate in the NSM to present your component library and technical readiness. Show the team the "UI Atoms" and the clickable "Hallways" you’ve built.

UI Delivery (PR & Merge): Commit your foundational components and navigation routes to your branch. Create a Pull Request (PR) and merge it into main via the mentor buttons panel. This locks in the UI architecture before the build phase begins.

Cards.Name: Staff Input Interface
Cards.Description:
This week, you serve as the project’s Strategic Pulse. 
 
 Your mission is to maintain a dual-stream focus: Internal Alignment to deeply understand the module’s technical evolution, and External Validation to ground that progress in operational reality. 
 
 You are responsible for ensuring that the Staff Input Interface is not just a technical achievement, but a user-friendly solution for daycare staff. 
 
 You will bridge the gap between the development team’s potential constraints and the actual operational habits of daycare staff.
ChecklistItems:
Staff Sync: At the start of the week, consult with the AI Customer to identify “unknowns” regarding data entry needs. 
 Focus: Clarify operational details that will affect the build (e.g., “Do staff usually log activities in real-time, or do they prefer batch entries at the end of the day?”).

Internal Product Alignment: Once the PM and UI/UX Designer have drafted the initial User Story and design, meet to conduct a deep-dive. 
 Goal: Ensure you can articulate exactly how the data entry logic and validations are intended to work before you head into the field.

Field Stakeholder Outreach: Conduct targeted interviews with 2-3 daycare staff members (e.g., teachers, caregivers, or administrators). 
 Validation: Present the high-level concept of the Staff Input Interface to gather raw, unbiased feedback.

Operational Habit Validation: Analyze whether the proposed data entry workflow (specifically the ease of logging attendance and activities) aligns with the “on-the-ground” behavior of busy daycare staff during a shift.

CRM Intelligence Logging: Use the CRM button in the Meeting Room to document all field insights and stakeholder profiles. 
 Data Management: Ensure you are managing the staff input list (entering/editing) so the entire team has a “Data Source of Truth” regarding daily activities.

Strategic Resource Management: Use the Resources button to upload and link relevant training materials, operational guidelines, or best practice examples. 
 Benchmark: Ensure the team has access to these references for inspiration.

Next Sprint Meeting (NSM): Present your “Field Intelligence” at the NSM. 
 Prioritization: Help the team prioritize data entry fields and validation logic based on real-world urgency versus technical “nice-to-haves.”
Sprint 4 Staggered Look-Ahead: Work with the PM to identify the key stakeholders needed for the Parent Dashboard, which will be your primary focus next week.

Cards.Name: Tactical Planning & Design Alignment
Cards.Description:
This week, you are the Bridge between the high-level journey and the actual build.

Your mission is to translate the vision from Sprint 1 into a detailed, actionable User Story for the Staff Input Interface.

Sprint 2 is about removing every technical and logical blocker before we start coding in Sprint 3.

You are the team's Unlocker.

We are focusing entirely on the "Input Foundation" - capturing the needs of diverse staff profiles like daycare workers, administrators, and support staff.

Success means staff can seamlessly log attendance, meals, activities, and any incidents via a user-friendly interface.

By the end of this week, the team must know exactly what to build, why, and how.

Every decision you document now regarding the "Staff Input Experience" ensures the developers and designers can execute the build with total focus and zero ambiguity.
ChecklistItems: Meeting Orchestration: Schedule a short Sprint 2 Status Meeting at the start of the week for team alignment. Schedule the Next Sprint Meeting (NSM) for the second half of the week.
Status Sync Recap: Publish a concise summary of the week's setup tasks and any immediate blockers identified during the status meeting to the Team Chat.

Customer Validation: Interview the AI Customer to finalize the required Staff Input fields (limit to max 6) and identify the specific IF/THEN validation rules, such as rules for attendance times or meal quantities.

The User Story Deep-Dive: Locate the existing Staff Input Interface card in the "User Stories" Trello list and fill it out completely: 
 (1) Description Field: Write the formal story ("As a... I want... so that..."), define the In-Scope/Out-of-Scope lists, and include a Data Table listing every Field Name, Field Type (Text, Number, etc.), and whether it is Required (Yes/No). 
 (2) Acceptance Criteria Checklist: Populate the pre-made, empty checklist on the card with specific "Done" conditions, for example, "Confirmation of successful data entry appears" or "Error messages trigger correctly for failed entries".

Design Alignment: Sync with the UI/UX Designer to review the Staff Input Interface Design and success screens. Ensure the final design, including the visual language, is saved in the Team Room before the sprint ends.

Technical Requirements Sync: Meet with the Backend Developer once your User Story is drafted. Help them identify the specific Tables and Fields needed to support the Staff Input logic based on your Data Specification.
Technical Readiness Audit: Verify the development environment. Confirm the BE has the Schema ready and has generated Mock Data, and that the FE has built the UI Atoms and Navigation Hallways.

Next Sprint Meeting (NSM): Lead the planning session. 
 Agenda: 
 (1) Present the User Story, Data Specs, and AC 
 (2) Facilitate T-shirt sizing (S/M/L) 
 (3) Confirm developer understanding 
 (4) Assign owners
Sprint 3 Launch Sync: Publish a consolidated summary of the build goals and a clear list of prioritized action items in the Team Chat to formally trigger the start of the execution phase.

Cards.Name: Staff Input Interface Development
Cards.Description:
This week, you will focus on creating an intuitive interface for daycare staff to efficiently log daily activities and care information for each child.

Your mission is to transform the initial concepts into a polished user interface that promotes ease of use and accuracy in data entry. By the end of this week, the development team should have a comprehensive design that includes all necessary fields for attendance, meals, activities, and more.

Your goal is to ensure the interface not only functions correctly but also feels professional, reliable, and accessible for all staff members from the very first interaction.
ChecklistItems: Logo & Visual Language: Interview the AI Customer regarding the branding elements for the Staff Input Interface, including logo guidelines and visual direction. Design the product logo and establish the visual language in Figma (e.g., Color Palette and Typography).
Staff Requirement Sync: Interview the AI Customer to identify specific user needs for the Staff Input Interface and verify any functional expectations.

PM Requirement Sync: Meet with the PM as soon as the Staff Input Interface user story is drafted. Review the required data fields and validation logic to ensure your design supports all business and technical constraints.
Interface Design: Create a mobile-first design of the Staff Input Interface in Figma. Focus on layout hierarchy and ease of use on smaller screens to ensure a smooth data entry experience for staff.

States & Notifications: Design success and error states for all input fields and add them to the Figma design system. Create the corresponding success and error notifications for the data entry process.

Technical Handshake: Sync with the Frontend Developer to review your designs. Ensure general synchronization regarding technical feasibility and confirm that your UI components align with the project’s component library.
Next Sprint Meeting (NSM): Participate in the NSM to present the final design to the team. This will serve as the “North Star” for the upcoming development in Sprint 3.
Sprint 4 Look-Ahead: Briefly coordinate with the Product Manager to identify the core requirements for the Parent Dashboard module. This will be your research focus starting next week.

Cards.Name: Feature Logic & API Execution
Cards.Description:
This is it. The Build Week. 
 
 You are no longer just an architect. You are the Data Entry Specialist. 
 
 Your mission is to build the interface and the storage that allow staff to efficiently record daily activities and care information for each child. 
 
 By the end of this sprint, when a staff member submits data on the interface, your backend must catch that data, verify it against the business rules, and save it permanently to the database. 
 
 You are responsible for ensuring that the data "Staff Input Interface" is secure, accurate, and ready for the next stage of the project.
ChecklistItems: Sprint 3 Launch: Participate in the Sprint Meeting led by the PM. Review the User Story and Acceptance Criteria to ensure you are 100% aligned on the build goals.
Environment Setup: Create your Sprint 3 branch via the mentor buttons panel to begin the build.
Database Migration: Implement the physical tables in the database based on the schema you finalized in Sprint 2. Ensure all fields (IDs, timestamps, and data types) are correctly configured.

Validation Engine Build: Code the validation logic as defined in the PM's User Story (e.g., ensuring activity entries include required fields or that daily care records are completed before submission).

API Endpoint Development: Build the POST endpoint that will receive the staff input data. Ensure the response body includes all necessary parameters for UI responsiveness (e.g., specific error codes, unique record IDs, or updated state objects).
Unit Testing: Write and execute unit tests for your validation logic and database service. Ensure that "bad data" is rejected with the correct error codes and "good data" is prepared for storage.
Peer Code Review: Share your code with the AI Mentor for a review session to ensure your logic is clean, secure, and follows best practices.

Integration Test (Internal): Use your "Mock Data" from last week to test the endpoint. Confirm that valid data is saved and invalid data triggers the correct error response codes before involving the frontend.

Frontend Handshake: Pair with the Frontend Developer to "wire up" the input fields. Verify that their UI components are successfully sending the data to your endpoint and receiving the expected response parameters.

Next Module Requirements Sync: Meet with the PM once the Parent Dashboard User Story is drafted to identify the specific tables and fields needed to support the upcoming logic based on the new Data Specification.
Technical Readiness Audit: Prepare the environment for the PM's audit by ensuring the Parent Dashboard schema is ready and comprehensive mock data has been generated for the next phase.

Next Sprint Meeting (NSM): Participate in the NSM to plan the development of the Parent Dashboard. This session serves as the primary planning phase for the upcoming sprint, though it can also be used to demonstrate that the input data is being successfully captured and stored.
Feature Shipping (PR & Merge): Push your final code, create a Pull Request (PR), and merge it into main via the mentor buttons panel to officially complete the Staff Input Interface.

Cards.Name: Staff Input Logic & Database Integration
Cards.Description:
This is the Build Week. 
 
 You are no longer just building components. You are building the Experience. 
 
 Your mission is to take the "Atoms" from Sprint 2 and wire them into a fully functional, responsive interface that handles staff input and server feedback. 
 
 By the end of this sprint, a staff member should be able to log daily activities, see real-time validation, and receive a clear success or error message from the server. 
 
 You are responsible for the "Handshake" between the staff and the data engine.
ChecklistItems: Sprint 3 Launch: Participate in the Sprint Meeting led by the PM. Review the User Story and Acceptance Criteria to ensure the input interface behavior matches the business requirements.
Environment Setup: Create your Sprint 3 branch via the mentor buttons panel to begin the build.

Temporary Access Point: Add a "Staff Input Interface" button to the index.html file "frontend-buttons" panel. This ensures the interface is accessible to all team members via the "Live Project" link during development. Note that a professional Landing Page will be properly designed toward the end of the project (Sprint 7).
Input Logic Implementation: Transform your static components into a dynamic input interface. Implement the local state management to capture staff input for all fields defined in the PM's Data Table.
Client-Side Validation: Code the immediate UI feedback logic (e.g., preventing empty submissions, checking time formats, or ensuring correct data entries) before the data even hits the server.
Database Integration (The Wiring): Connect your input interface to the Backend's POST endpoint. Implement the fetch or axios calls to send the staff data to the server.

Responsive Feedback & Error Handling: Use the server's response parameters to trigger UI changes. Build the logic for loading states, specific field error messages, and the confirmation of successful data entry.
Peer Code Review: Share your code with the AI Mentor for a review session to ensure your state management is efficient and your input logic is robust.
Integration Test (Internal): Run a full end-to-end test of the flow. Confirm that the input interface correctly handles both a "Happy Path" (success) and "Edge Cases" (validation errors).
Backend Handshake: Pair with the Backend Developer to verify that your "Request" body perfectly matches their endpoint's expectations and that you are correctly parsing their "Response" parameters.

Next Module Requirements Sync: Meet with the PM once the Daily Activity Management User Story is drafted to identify any unique input requirements, table structures, or navigation hallways needed for the next phase.
Technical Readiness Audit: Prepare the environment for the PM's audit by ensuring the Parent Dashboard atoms and navigation hallways are built and ready for the Sprint 4 integration.

Next Sprint Meeting (NSM): Participate in the NSM to plan the development of the Parent Dashboard. This session serves as the primary planning phase for the upcoming sprint, though it can also be used to demonstrate the live input flow and successful navigation to the confirmation screen.
Feature Shipping (PR & Merge): Push your final code, create a Pull Request (PR), and merge it into main via the mentor buttons panel to officially complete the Input Experience.

Cards.Name: Strategic Pulse and Management Validation
Cards.Description:
This week, you serve as the project’s Strategic Pulse.

Your mission is to maintain a dual-stream focus: Internal Alignment to deeply understand the next phase of the product’s technical evolution, and External Validation to ground the upcoming Parent Dashboard module in market reality.

You are responsible for ensuring that the dashboard experience is not just a technical interface, but a tool that fits the high-speed operational needs of parents overseeing their child's daily experiences.

You will bridge the gap between the development team’s potential constraints and the actual operational habits of parents, staff, and caregivers.
ChecklistItems: Customer Sync: At the start of the week, consult with the AI Customer to identify "unknowns" regarding the Parent Dashboard module. Clarify management details that will affect the build, such as how parents prefer to navigate updates and view detailed insights.

Internal Product Alignment: Once the PM and UI/UX Designer have drafted the initial User Story and design for the Parent Dashboard module, meet to conduct a deep-dive. Ensure you can articulate exactly how the dashboard logic and notification transitions are intended to work.
Field Stakeholder Outreach: Conduct targeted interviews with 2-3 potential "Parents" or "Caregivers". Present the high-level concept of the dashboard to gather raw, unbiased feedback.

Operational Habit Validation: Analyze whether the proposed digital dashboard workflow (specifically the data presentation and notification system) aligns with the "on-the-ground" behavior of a parent managing their child's daily updates.

CRM Intelligence Logging: Use the CRM button in the Meeting Room to document all dashboard-related insights and stakeholder profiles. Ensure you are managing the prospect list so the team has a "Data Source of Truth" regarding the needs of parents.

Strategic Resource Management: Use the Resources button to upload and link relevant market research regarding parent dashboards or child monitoring software. Ensure the team has access to these benchmarks for inspiration.

Next Sprint Meeting (NSM): Present your "Field Intelligence" at the NSM. Help the team prioritize dashboard features and information flow based on real-world parental urgency versus technical "nice-to-haves."
Sprint 5 Staggered Look-Ahead: Work with the PM to identify the key stakeholders needed for the validation of the AI Monitoring and Insight Agent, which will be your primary focus next week.

Cards.Name: Tactical Blueprint and User Story
Cards.Description:
This week, you are the Bridge between the current build and the next phase of the project.

Your mission is to translate the vision from the global roadmap into a detailed, actionable User Story for the Parent Dashboard module.

Sprint 3 is about removing every technical and logical blocker before we start coding in Sprint 4.

You are the team’s Unlocker.

We are shifting focus from data entry to data management, exploring how parents will interact with, process, and oversee the information regarding their child's daily experiences.

Success means defining a clear, actionable workflow that transforms raw data into organized, manageable outcomes for parents.

By the end of this week, the team must know exactly what to build for the next module, why, and how.

Every decision you document now regarding the Parent Dashboard ensures the developers and designers can execute the build with total focus and zero ambiguity.
ChecklistItems: Meeting Orchestration: Schedule a short Sprint 3 Status Meeting at the start of the week for team alignment. Schedule the Next Sprint Meeting (NSM) for the second half of the week.
Status Sync Recap: Publish a concise summary of the week’s setup tasks and any immediate blockers identified during the status meeting to the Team Chat.
Customer Validation: Interview the AI Customer to finalize the required dashboard views (Daily Summary vs. Detailed View) and identify the specific business logic for the Notifications flow.

The User Story Deep-Dive: Locate the existing Parent Dashboard card in the “User Stories” Trello list and fill it out completely: 
 (1) Description Field: Write the formal story (“As a... I want... so that...” ), define the In-Scope/Out-of-Scope lists, and include a Data Table listing every Field Name (e.g., Meals, Naps, Activities), Data Type, and required User Actions (e.g., View, Acknowledge). 
 (2) Acceptance Criteria Checklist: Populate the pre-made, empty checklist on the card with specific “Done” conditions, such as real-time data updates and validated logic for notifications.

Design Alignment: Sync with the UI/UX Designer to review the interface designs and detail views. Ensure the final design, including the visual language, is saved in the Team Room before the sprint ends.

Technical Requirements Sync: Meet with the Backend Developer once your User Story is drafted. Help them identify the specific Tables and Fields needed to support the Parent Dashboard logic based on your Data Specification.

Technical Readiness Audit: Verify the development environment. Confirm the BE has the Schema ready and has generated Mock Data, and that the FE has built the UI Atoms and Navigation Hallways for the dashboard.

Next Sprint Meeting (NSM): Lead the planning session. 
 Agenda: 
 (1) Present the User Story, Data Specs, and AC 
 (2) Facilitate T-shirt sizing (S/M/L) 
 (3) Confirm developer understanding of the dashboard logic 
 (4) Assign owners for Sprint 4
Sprint 4 Launch Sync: Publish a consolidated summary of the build goals and a clear list of prioritized action items in the Team Chat to formally trigger the start of the execution phase.

Cards.Name: Detailed Design and Management Experience
Cards.Description:
This week, you expand the visual ecosystem.

Your mission is to move from the core brand elements of Sprint 2 into a high-fidelity dashboard experience for the Parent Dashboard module.

You are the Guardian of the User Experience.

By the end of this week, the developers should have a "Design Source of Truth" including the layouts for the information sections, detail views, and interactive elements for parent engagement.

API Endpoint Development: Build the GET endpoints for the dashboard views, alongside the PATCH/PUT endpoints to handle updates. Ensure the response body includes the updated state objects for UI responsiveness.
Unit Testing: Write and execute unit tests for your retrieval logic and update functions. Ensure that unauthorized or invalid state changes are rejected with the correct error codes.

Integration Test (Internal): Use your "Mock Data" from Sprint 3 to verify the dashboard endpoints. Confirm that the filters return the correct data and that updates persist in the database before involving the frontend.

Interaction & Feedback Handling: Build the logic for interactive data states. Ensure the UI provides clear feedback during data fetching (loading states) and handles empty result sets or filtering errors gracefully.
Peer Code Review: Share your code with the AI Mentor for a review session to ensure your data mapping logic is efficient and your chart components are performant.

Integration Test (Internal): Run a full end-to-end test of the reporting flow. Confirm that the interface correctly renders aggregated data and that all filters trigger the expected updates from the API.
Backend Handshake: Pair with the Backend Developer to verify that the aggregated data format served by their endpoints perfectly matches the expectations of your visualization components.

Next Module Requirements Sync: Meet with the PM once the Application Landing Page requirements are drafted to identify the specific visual assets, branding elements, and navigation links needed for the final phase.
Technical Readiness Audit: Prepare the environment for the PM’s audit by ensuring the structural "Atoms" for the Landing Page (hero sections, feature cards) are ready for the Sprint 7 build.

Next Sprint Meeting (NSM): Participate in the NSM to plan the development of the Application Landing Page. This session serves as the primary planning phase for the final project sprint, though it can also be used to demonstrate the functional Analytics Hub.
Feature Shipping (PR & Merge): Push your final code, create a Pull Request (PR), and merge it into main via the mentor buttons panel to officially complete the Reports experience.

Cards.Name: Strategic Logic and Impact-ROI Modeling
Cards.Description:
This week, you are transitioning the project from a "functional application" to a "measurable socio-economic solution." 
 
 Your mission is to develop a comprehensive Logic Model that maps the causal link between our technological inputs and the tangible social and economic value we create. 
 
 You are the team's Value Architect. 
 
 Success means establishing a quantitative framework that proves how the platform optimizes resources. By the end of this week, you will define the "Value Equation" to serve as the core data for investor pitches. All finalized documentation must be saved under the Resources section in the Team Room.
ChecklistItems: Logic Model Framework (Inputs & Outputs): Define the resources invested and map them to the platform’s technical deliverables (e.g., reporting tools, algorithms), ensuring the Strategic Logic Model Document is finalized and saved to the Resources folder.

Impact Analysis (Outcomes): Identify meaningful outcomes reflected in the reports, such as improvements in children’s daily routines, participation patterns, and developmental progress, to provide a clear narrative of how daycare activities support wellbeing and care quality.

Economic ROI Modeling (Business Stakeholders): Analyze financial benefits for participating businesses, including tax incentives, operational savings, and brand equity, and save the finalized ROI & Business Value Analysis to the Resources section.

Municipal Value Proposition: Estimate how improved reporting and analytics can support better planning of daycare services, attendance monitoring, and child development tracking, strengthening long term collaboration and service quality for municipalities.
The "Value Equation" Synthesis: Formulate a high-impact KPI (e.g., 3.5x ROI) to demonstrate efficiency and document the Executive Summary: The Value Equation in the Resources section.
Pitch Material Integration: Translate the Logic Model and ROI data into high-impact slides, ensuring the Updated Investor Pitch Deck is saved under Resources for upcoming presentations.
Stakeholder Validation: Meet with the AI Customer to verify that the ROI assumptions align with actual operational costs and budget categories.
Next Sprint Meeting (NSM): Present the completed Logic Model and ROI data to the team to align the marketing narrative with the new quantitative data.

Cards.Name: Tactical Blueprint and Operational Insights
Cards.Description:
This week, you are managing the transition toward a public-facing platform and the integration of the Speech-to-Text Service.

Your mission is to define the requirements for two key areas: the Application Landing page and the Speech-to-Text Service integration.

Sprint 6 is about resolving logical and technical dependencies before the final execution in Sprint 7.

You are the team’s Unlocker.

Success means establishing a clear structure for the landing page that effectively communicates the platform's purpose and a functional logic for the Speech-to-Text service that provides actionable insights for operational planning.

By the end of this week, the team must have the data specifications and user flows necessary to implement these features without technical ambiguity.
ChecklistItems: Meeting Orchestration: Schedule a short Sprint 6 Status Meeting at the start of the week for team alignment. Schedule the Next Sprint Meeting (NSM) for the second half of the week.
Status Sync Recap: Publish a concise summary of the week’s setup tasks and any immediate blockers identified during the status meeting to the Team Chat.

Customer Validation: Interview the AI Customer to finalize the primary messaging for the Landing Page and confirm the simple location insights the service should provide, such as estimated travel times and basic alerts for recurring late arrivals based on the available data.

The User Story (Speech-to-Text Service): Locate the dedicated card in the Trello “User Stories” list and fill it out. Draft a general narrative that defines the logic and criteria the service will use to capture voice recordings during daily activities and convert them to text. The story should outline how the system processes staff voice input to provide accurate, timely records for operational planning.

Design Alignment: Sync with the UI/UX Designer to review the high-fidelity Landing Page designs. Ensure the visual branding and content hierarchy are finalized and saved in the Team Room before the sprint ends.

Technical Requirements Sync: Meet with the Backend Developer to define the Integration Path with the Speech-to-Text API. Identify how the backend will process voice recordings and log the transcribed insights within the system architecture.

Technical Readiness Audit: Verify the development environment. Confirm the BE has the schema updated for voice recording and transcription logging, and the FE has prepared the structural containers for the Landing Page sections.

Next Sprint Meeting (NSM): Lead the final planning session for the project build. 
 Agenda: 
 (1) Present the Landing Page and Google Speach-to-Text Service User Stories 
 (2) Facilitate T-shirt sizing (S/M/L) for the remaining tasks 
 (3) Confirm developer understanding of the api integration 
 (4) Assign owners for the final Sprint 7 build phase
Sprint 7 Launch Sync: Publish a consolidated summary of the final build goals and a list of prioritized action items in the Team Chat to formally trigger the start of the execution phase.

Cards.Name: The Public Face - Landing Page Design
Cards.Description:
This week, you move from internal operational tools to the public-facing storefront of the platform.

Your mission is to design the high-fidelity Application Landing Page that will present the digital contact notebook and its key capabilities to parents, daycare staff, and partners.

You are the Guardian of the Brand.

By the end of this week, you must provide the developers with a "Visual Source of Truth" that defines the platform’s value proposition, core features, and call-to-action flow. Your goal is to ensure the first impression of the platform is professional, trustworthy, and clearly communicates the mission of improving transparency and care tracking in special daycare settings.
ChecklistItems: Customer Requirement Sync: Interview the AI Customer to finalize the primary messaging, value propositions, and the target audience priorities for the landing page.

PM Requirement Sync: Meet with the PM as soon as the Landing Page user story is drafted. Review the defined content hierarchy and acceptance criteria to ensure your visual strategy is perfectly aligned with the project requirements.

Landing Page Design (High-Fidelity): Create the comprehensive layout in Figma. 
 (1) Hero Section: Design the primary visual hook and headline area to grab immediate attention. 
 (2) Feature Highlights: Create the visual framework for demonstrating the platform's core modules (Intake, Dashboard, Insights). 
 (3) Impact Metrics: Design the section for displaying community impact data and social proof to build trust.
Call-to-Action (CTA) Design: Establish the visual hierarchy for buttons and links. Ensure the primary conversion paths (e.g., "View Daily Reports," "Explore Features") are prominent and intuitive.
Responsive Design Framework: Define how the landing page scales across mobile, tablet, and desktop viewports to ensure a seamless experience for all visitors.

Technical Handshake: Sync with the Frontend Developer to review the landing page designs. 
 Synchronization: Confirm the feasibility of the proposed layouts and asset types. 
 Feasibility: Ensure the design components are optimized for fast loading and mobile performance.

Next Sprint Meeting (NSM): Participate in the NSM to present the finalized Landing Page designs to the team. 
 Agenda: 
 (1) Present the Branding and Landing Page layouts 
 (2) Demonstrate the visitor journey and CTA flow 
 (3) Confirm design handoff for the Sprint 7 "Grand Finale" build

Cards.Name: Final Feature Integration
Cards.Description:
This is the final feature sprint. Your mission is to complete the technical core of the Google Speech-to-Text Service by implementing a backend add-on that improves the staff input experience, allowing real-time voice capture and transcription of daily activities for children in special daycare settings.

Your focus is on ensuring the speech-to-text logic is reliable and the system architecture is fully operational for its primary functional debut. You are the System Anchor.

Success means a module with fully operational backend voice capture and transcription logic that is integrated, tested, and merged. By the end of this week, the feature set must be 100% complete and documented.
ChecklistItems: Sprint 7 Launch: Participate in the Sprint Meeting led by the PM. Review the User Story and Acceptance Criteria to ensure you are 100% aligned on the logistics build goals.
Environment Setup: Create your Sprint 7 branch via the mentor buttons panel to begin the build.

Speech-to-Text Integration: Implement the logic for the Google Speech-to-Text Service. Create the endpoint to receive real-time voice recordings from staff and integrate the transcription engine to return accurate text entries for daily activity logs.

Voice Data Integration: Update the relevant APIs to incorporate the speech-to-text insights, ensuring they are fully integrated into the existing staff input form. Transcribed text should display seamlessly alongside the child’s activity and attendance data.
Peer Code Review: Conduct a thorough review of the speech-to-text logic with the AI Mentor. Focus on the accuracy of the transcriptions and the efficiency of the data logging before the final merge.

Final PR & Merge: Complete the final Pull Request for all Sprint 7 features. Conduct a rigorous code review and merge the branch into the main repository once all quality checks are passed to establish the definitive feature-complete codebase.

Project Handoff Documentation: Finalize the API Documentation and System Architecture Map for the Speech-to-Text Service, ensuring all finalized technical docs are saved under the Resources section in the Team Room.
Next Sprint Meeting (NSM): Participate in the NSM to demonstrate the new integrated service to the team and confirm the feature set is complete.

Cards.Name: Final Feature Integration
Cards.Description:
This is the final feature sprint. Your mission is to bring the platform to life by building the public-facing Landing Page and finalizing the frontend architecture.

You are the bridge between the project’s strategic value and the user’s experience. You are moving from internal tools to a polished, professional landing page that clearly communicates the platform’s mission of improving care transparency and supporting parents and daycare staff.

Success means a pixel-perfect, responsive landing page that serves as the gateway for all stakeholders. By the end of this week, the frontend feature set must be 100% complete and documented.
ChecklistItems: Sprint 7 Launch: Participate in the Sprint Meeting led by the PM. Review the User Story and Acceptance Criteria to ensure you are 100% aligned on the build goals.
Environment Setup: Create your Sprint 7 branch via the mentor buttons panel to begin the build.

Landing Page Implementation: Build the high-fidelity, responsive Landing Page in React based on the finalized Figma designs. Ensure the Hero section, Feature Highlights, and Impact Metrics are visually consistent and optimized for all screen sizes. Once completed, replace the existing index.html file with this final implementation.

Peer Code Review: Conduct a thorough review of the Landing Page code with the AI Mentor. Focus on component reusability, CSS responsiveness, and alignment with the UI Documentation before proceeding to the final merge.

Final PR & Merge: Complete the final Pull Request for all Sprint 7 features. Conduct a thorough code review and merge the branch into the main repository once all quality checks are passed to establish the definitive feature-complete codebase.
Project Handoff Documentation: Finalize the Frontend Component Library and UI Documentation, ensuring all finalized technical docs are saved under the Resources section in the Team Room.
Next Sprint Meeting (NSM): Participate in the NSM to demonstrate the live Landing Page to the team and confirm the frontend feature set is complete.

Cards.Name: The "Go-to-Market" Launch Campaign
Cards.Description:
This is the final push. Your mission is to lead a collaborative "Marketing Task Force" alongside the UI/UX Designer and the PM to launch the platform into the wild. 
 
 You are moving from the "building" phase to the "telling" phase. This sprint is entirely focused on creating the narrative and visual tools required to convince the world that this solution isn't just a project, it’s an essential community asset. Since the three of you are operating as a single unit, you will decide internally how to distribute the workload, but the goal remains the same: a high-impact, professional suite of marketing assets. 
 
 Success means having a cohesive brand presence across all platforms. By the end of this week, all campaign materials must be finalized and accessible in the Team Room.
ChecklistItems: Marketing Strategy Sync: Convene with the PM and UI/UX Designer to finalize the core campaign message. Ensure the strategy effectively bridges technical features with the economic ROI to create a compelling "why now" for potential partners and donors.

Social Media Campaign: Design and draft a set of high-impact posts for LinkedIn and Instagram that showcase the platform in action, ensuring that the link to the finalized Social Media Content Calendar and Assets is saved under the Resources section.

Final Pitch Deck: Refine the presentation for online delivery, focusing on the story of the digital contact notebook and the data-driven impact on child care and operational insights, and save the link to the Online Presentation Deck under the Resources section in the Team Room.

YouTube Promotional Video: Develop the script and storyboard for a professional explainer movie that highlights the user journey and the platform's value, ensuring the link to the YouTube Script and Storyboard is saved under the Resources section.

Impact Storytelling (Success Story): Transform a customer interview or use case into a narrative-driven success story that humanizes the platform's benefits, ensuring the link to this Impact Case Study is saved under the Resources section.

Cross-Functional Asset Audit: Conduct a final review of all marketing materials as a team to ensure that the messaging from the PM, the visuals from UI/UX, and the strategy from BizDev are perfectly aligned for a unified brand voice.

Final Resource Consolidation: Verify that the links for every piece of the "Go-to-Market" puzzle are organized, labeled, and securely listed under the Resources section of the Team Room for the project's official conclusion.

Cards.Name: Stabilization & Final Polish
Cards.Description:
Your mission is to switch from feature building to a collaborative "Bug Hunting" phase alongside the entire team to ensure the platform is production-ready. 
 
 You are moving from the "feature" phase to the "quality" phase. This sprint is entirely focused on identifying glitches and ensuring the system logic is flawless before the final handoff. Since everyone is doing QA, you will monitor the Meeting Room for every "Bugs case" logged and address them in your dedicated branch. 
 
 Success means a robust, error-free backend that has been fully merged into the main repository.
ChecklistItems: [ ] Sprint 8 Launch: Participate in the final Sprint Planning meeting led by the PM. Review the QA process and the "Bugs" sprint objectives to ensure alignment on the stabilization goals.
[ ] Environment Setup: Create your dedicated "Bugs" branch via the mentor buttons panel. This will be the single point of truth for all code fixes during this sprint.

[ ] Team-Wide QA & Bug Discovery: Join the entire team in a comprehensive QA cycle. Monitor the Meeting Room for any new Bugs case logged by the PM, BizDev, or designers, and verify each against the backend logic.
[ ] Bug Resolution: Systematically address every Bugs case related to the backend. Focus on API stability, data validation, and logic errors to ensure the system is robust.
[ ] Peer Bug Review: Conduct a thorough review of your fixes with the AI Mentor. Ensure that bug resolutions are optimized and do not introduce new regressions into the established feature set.

[ ] Final PR & Merge: Complete the final Pull Request for the "Bugs" branch. Conduct a final rigorous code review and merge the stabilized code into the main repository to establish the project's definitive version.
[ ] Project Summary Meeting: Participate in the final meeting led by the PM to review the project's overall journey, celebrate the successful build, and confirm the platform is stable.

Cards.Name: Stabilization & Project Summary
Cards.Description:
Your mission is to switch from the marketing launch to a collaborative "Bug Hunting" phase alongside the entire team to ensure the platform is production-ready. 
 
 You are moving from the "telling" phase to the "quality and closure" phase. This sprint is entirely focused on identifying glitches and ensuring the business logic and user experience are flawless before the final handoff. Since everyone is doing QA, you will monitor the platform as a user and log every "Bugs case" in the Meeting room. 
 
 Success means a stable platform and a finalized set of marketing materials that perfectly reflect the project's final state for the Project Summary meeting.
ChecklistItems: [ ] Sprint 8 Launch: Participate in the final Sprint Planning meeting led by the PM. Review the QA process and understand how to log and track issues during this stabilization week.

[ ] Team-Wide QA & Logging: Dive into the platform from a "customer" perspective. Conduct thorough testing of the user journeys and log every Bugs case you discover in the Meeting room for the developers to address.

[ ] Marketing Asset Alignment: Review the Pitch Deck, Social Media posts, and YouTube Video created in Sprint 7. Ensure they reflect the final version of the product and update any links or screenshots if necessary.

[ ] Project Summary Preparation: Prepare your highlights and "lessons learned" for the final Project Summary meeting. Be ready to share the business impact and the growth potential of the project with the team.
[ ] Final Resource Audit: Conduct a final check of the Resources folder to ensure all marketing links and business documentation are organized and accessible for the final project closure.

Cards.Name: Project Summary & Quality Assurance
Cards.Description:
This is the final stabilization push. Your mission is to coordinate the collaborative "Bug Hunting" phase alongside the entire team while preparing the final project closure. 
 
 You are moving from the "oversight" phase to the "quality and closure" phase. This sprint is entirely focused on identifying every glitch and facilitating the final Project Summary. Since everyone is doing QA, you will lead by example, logging every "Bugs case" in the Meeting room and ensuring the developers are unblocked to resolve them. 
 
 Success means a stable platform and a team that is fully aligned on the project's achievements during the final summary meeting.
ChecklistItems: [ ] Sprint 8 Launch: Lead the final Sprint Planning meeting. Define the QA protocols and set the expectations for the "Bugs case" logging process to ensure the team is synced for the final week.

[ ] Team-Wide QA & Logging: Dive into the platform as a user. Conduct thorough end-to-end testing and log every Bugs case discovered in the Meeting room, ensuring each report is clear and actionable for the developers.

[ ] Trello Bug Management: Manage the Bugs case queue exclusively via Trello (PM access only). Assign the priority and severity for each logged issue to ensure the developers are focused on the most critical stability tasks first.

[ ] Developer Coordination: Monitor the progress of the dedicated "Bugs" branches. Ensure that both the Backend and Frontend developers complete their final PR and merge to the main repository by the end of the sprint.

[ ] Project Summary Meeting Organization: Schedule and prepare the agenda for the final Project Summary meeting. Ensure all team members are invited to share their highlights, challenges, and final results.

[ ] Final Project Wrap-up: Conduct a final audit of the Resources folder in the Team Room. Verify that all sprint deliverables, marketing assets, and technical docs (links) are correctly organized for the final handoff.
[ ] Project Summary Presentation: Lead the final team session. Celebrate the successful build, review the "Bugs case" resolution metrics, and officially conclude the project.

 Success means a pixel-perfect, stable platform and a finalized brand presence for the Project Summary meeting.

[ ] Team-Wide QA & Logging: Dive into the platform as a user. Conduct thorough "visual QA" and log every Bugs case you discover in the Meeting room, focusing on spacing, alignment, and interaction flows.

[ ] Visual Integrity Check: Monitor the bug fixes being implemented by developers. Ensure that no fix accidentally breaks the official color palette, logo placement, or typography standards established for the project.

[ ] Marketing Asset Alignment: Review the Pitch Deck, Social Media graphics, and YouTube Video. Ensure they reflect the final state of the platform and replace any outdated screenshots if bug fixes have changed the UI.
[ ] Project Summary Preparation: Prepare your highlights and "design evolution" notes for the final Project Summary meeting. Be ready to showcase how the UI/UX helped achieve the project goals.
[ ] Final Resource Audit: Conduct a final check of the Resources folder to ensure all design links, brand assets, and marketing visuals are organized and accessible for the final project closure.
